planting.note: Transplant small clumps with intact rhizomes in early fall in moist, well-drained, alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; liverleaf is slow-growing and very long-lived, preferring undisturbed conditions once established.

directions: Transplant small clumps in early fall in moist, well-drained, alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; liverleaf (hepatica) is one of the earliest native wildflowers to bloom in eastern North American woodlands — its lavender-blue to white, delicate, many-stamened flowers push directly through the leaf litter in late winter to early spring, often before any other woodland wildflower. The three-lobed, leathery leaves persist through winter, often turning deep purplish-bronze in cold weather, and are replaced by fresh green leaves after bloom. Very slow-growing but extremely long-lived, persisting undisturbed for decades in suitable woodland conditions. Requires alkaline to neutral soil (unlike most woodland natives that prefer acid), making it suitable for limestone-rich areas. Nearly impossible to buy and best obtained from native plant seed exchanges.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; this species (P. stolonifera) is the shade-tolerant creeping phlox, distinct from P. subulata which requires full sun — always confirm species before planting in shade.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creeping phlox (Phlox stolonifera) is a native Appalachian woodland groundcover distinctly different from the sun-loving moss phlox (P. subulata) — P. stolonifera thrives in partial to full shade, producing spreading mats of rounded, evergreen leaves with runners that root at the nodes and producing saucer-shaped, fragrant flowers in violet-blue, pink, or white in mid-spring. After bloom, the mat provides attractive, year-round groundcover under trees and in shaded woodland beds. Much better for shade than the commonly sold P. subulata. 'Blue Ridge' (blue-violet), 'Pink Ridge' (rose-pink), and 'Sherwood Purple' are excellent named cultivars. Essentially maintenance-free once established in suitable conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in early fall; white baneberry is extremely poisonous — all parts, especially the berries, are toxic to humans and pets.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; white baneberry (doll's eyes) is a native eastern North American woodland perennial grown for its dramatic ornamental interest rather than any culinary use — the plant is highly poisonous in all parts. In late spring it produces a fluffy cluster of small white flowers, but the main event is the fruit display in late summer: glistening white berries, each marked with a distinctive black dot exactly like a doll's eye, borne on vivid red-pink stalks — a striking and surreal combination that is one of the most visually arresting effects in the shade garden. The berries are extremely toxic and very attractive to children — plant with awareness. Excellent for bold, late-season interest in shaded woodland gardens where other plants are fading.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in early fall; American spikenard is a bold, large perennial that requires ample space — allow 4–5 feet in every direction for a mature clump.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; American spikenard is a large, architectural native perennial of rich eastern forests that provides bold textural presence where scale plants are needed — the enormous, compound leaves with multiple pairs of leaflets can span 5 feet across, creating a tropical-looking foliage mass in the shade garden. Clusters of small, greenish-white flowers appear in midsummer on branched panicles, followed by clusters of dark reddish-purple to black berries that birds relish. The aromatic roots have a history of culinary and medicinal use in indigenous traditions and have been used as a flavoring similar to sarsaparilla. Dies to the ground in winter. Excellent as a bold accent in large-scale woodland garden compositions and native plant restorations.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in water or moist perlite, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil or potting mix in bright indirect light to partial shade; the intense purple color develops best in bright indirect light — direct sun scorches the leaves.

directions: Root stem cuttings easily in water or moist perlite; purple passion plant is a Javanese native grown for its extraordinary, intensely purple-violet velvety foliage — the dark-green, toothed leaves are so thickly covered with purple hairs that the entire plant appears to glow with deep, jewel-like purple coloration, a dramatic and unusual effect. The color is most intense in young growth and in bright indirect light; older leaves tend toward green. Pinch regularly to maintain compact, bushy form and prevent the plant from becoming leggy. The orange flowers that occasionally appear are unpleasant-smelling and should be removed — they signal the plant is aging. Excellent in combination plantings with ferns and silver-leaved companions in shaded patios and as an indoor accent plant. Roots in water readily for propagation.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
